---
title: "Quarto 5 â€” Revealjs Presentations"
subtitle: "Build slide decks from .qmd"
author: "R. Burke Squires"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
---

## Goals for this module

In the next 60 minutes you will:

- Convert a Quarto document into a slide deck with one YAML change.
- Use the level-2 heading rule to create new slides.
- Add speaker notes, incremental reveals, columns, and code highlighting.
- Customize the look with the built-in themes.

You will end this module with a working presentation that you could give tomorrow.

## The smallest possible deck

Take this entire file:

```yaml
---
title: "My talk"
format: revealjs
---

## Slide 1

Hello world.

## Slide 2

Goodbye world.
```

Render it. You have a slide deck. Press space to advance, `s` to open speaker notes, `f` for full screen, `?` for keyboard shortcuts.

::: {.callout-tip}
## The heading rule
By default, each level-2 heading (`##`) starts a new slide. Anything between two `##` lines is the content of one slide. Level-1 headings (`#`) create **section dividers** (a big colored title slide).
:::

## Theme

Revealjs ships with a dozen themes. Set one in YAML:

```yaml
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
```

Built-in themes: `beige`, `blood`, `dark`, `default`, `league`, `moon`, `night`, `serif`, `simple`, `sky`, `solarized`, `white`.

For corporate or institutional looks, define your own:

```yaml
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, my-niehs-theme.scss]
```

A `.scss` theme file lets you override colors and fonts. The workshop slides use a custom NIEHS theme in `slides/niehs-theme.scss` â€” look at it for a working example.

## Slide layout patterns

Here are the patterns you will use 95% of the time. Each one is shown as it would appear in a `.qmd` source.

### Title slide

```markdown
---
title: "Title of the talk"
subtitle: "A subtitle"
author: "R. Burke Squires"
institute: "NIEHS"
date: "2026-05-19"
format: revealjs
---
```

Quarto auto-generates a title slide from the YAML.

### Section divider

```markdown
# Part 1: Setup
```

A single `#` heading creates a full-screen section break.

### Bullet slide with incremental reveal

```markdown
## Three things to know

::: {.incremental}
- Quarto generalizes R Markdown.
- It speaks multiple languages.
- It produces many output formats.
:::
```

Each bullet appears on click. The `::: {.incremental}` div is the magic.

### Two-column slide

```markdown
## Tradeoffs

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Pros**

- Fast
- Cheap
- Reproducible
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Cons**

- Learning curve
- More files to track
:::

::::
```

### Slide with code that highlights

```markdown
## Loading data

```{r}
#| code-line-numbers: "1|2-3|4"

library(dplyr)
aq <- read.csv("airquality.csv")
aq <- na.omit(aq)
summary(aq)
```
```

The `code-line-numbers` option steps through the code one line at a time as you click. The string is a sequence of line ranges separated by `|`. Each step highlights a different set of lines.

### Slide with a figure that fills the screen

```markdown
## A picture

![](path/to/figure.png){.r-stretch}
```

The `.r-stretch` class makes the figure expand to fill available space.

### Speaker notes

```markdown
## A normal slide

Visible content goes here.

::: {.notes}
These notes only appear in the speaker view (press `s` during the talk).
:::
```

## Tasteful customization

A few YAML options worth knowing:

```yaml
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true        # show "5/24" in the corner
    chalkboard: true          # press 'b' to draw on the slide
    incremental: false        # default: bullets appear all at once
    transition: slide         # how slides transition: none, fade, slide, convex, concave, zoom
    code-overflow: wrap       # don't make code horizontally scroll
    smaller: false            # set true for global smaller text on every slide
    scrollable: false         # set true to allow per-slide scrolling
    progress: true            # show a progress bar at the bottom
    logo: niehs-logo.png      # small logo in corner
    footer: "Quarto Workshop Â· NIEHS Â· 2026"
```

The `chalkboard` feature is genuinely useful in training â€” you can annotate slides live during the talk and even save the annotations.

## Live demo

::: {.callout-important}
## Open the workshop slide deck now
Open `slides/05_revealjs_demo.qmd` (we will create this together). This is a real, working slide deck rendered to revealjs. Look at the YAML at the top, then scroll through and notice how each `##` heading maps to a slide. The lecturer will demo this live.
:::

## Exercise 4.1 â€” Convert and customize

Take the file you started in Module 1 and:

1. Change the YAML to `format: revealjs` (delete the `html:` block).
2. Render and step through the result.
3. Pick a theme that you like.
4. Find one bullet list in your file and wrap it in `::: {.incremental}` ... `:::`.
5. Add a section divider using `#` somewhere.
6. Add a speaker note using `::: {.notes}` ... `:::`.

## Exercise 4.2 â€” Code highlighting

Find any code chunk in your slides. Add `#| code-line-numbers: "1|2|3"` to it (or whatever line numbers you have). Render and step through the slide â€” each click should highlight the next line.

## A subtle point about chunks in slides

In a slide deck, **a single chunk lives on a single slide**. If your code is too long to fit on one slide, either:

- Break it into multiple chunks across multiple slides, or
- Add `scrollable: true` to that one slide:

```markdown
## A long script {.scrollable}

```{r}
# 50 lines of code...
```
```

You will see `.scrollable` and `.smaller` used as per-slide modifiers attached to the heading. They are class names â€” Quarto picks them up and applies styles only to that slide.

## Common revealjs problems

| Symptom | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Slides too crowded | Too much per slide | Break with more `##` headings |
| Code overflows off-screen | Long lines | `code-overflow: wrap` in YAML |
| Figure is tiny | No size set | `{.r-stretch}` or set `fig-width`/`fig-height` |
| Bullets all appear at once | Forgot incremental | Wrap in `::: {.incremental}` |
| Custom theme not loading | Wrong path | Use relative path from the `.qmd` file |

## What's next

The final teaching module covers **Quarto projects** â€” multi-document collections like websites and books â€” and **publishing** the things you've built.

Open [`06_parameterized_reports.qmd`](06_parameterized_reports.qmd).
